However, at the same time, the enforcement力度 ​​and specific rules of different jurisdictions still differ (for example, the US pursues criminal prosecution of violators, the EU highly emphasizes unified regulations, and Asia focuses on licensing management), requiring companies to consider the regulatory details of each region when formulating global compliance strategies. 2.2 Segregation of Client Assets Segregation of client assets is one of the core mechanisms in financial regulation to protect investor interests and prevent the transmission of institutional bankruptcy risks. In the traditional securities and futures field, various countries have long had mature rules (such as the US SEC's Client Funds Protection Rules and the UK FCA's Client Assets Rules) to ensure that brokers keep client funds separate from their own funds. For cryptocurrency trading platforms, a series of events in recent years (including the collapse of a global exchange at the end of 2022 due to misappropriation of customer funds) have increasingly highlighted the importance of segregation of customer assets. Regulators in various jurisdictions generally recognize that platforms must not commingle the digital assets held by users with their own assets, and are even more prohibited from using customer assets for lending or investment without authorization. Otherwise, if the platform experiences a financial crisis, it will seriously damage the rights and interests of investors. This has become one of the regulatory red lines in major jurisdictions. The European Union has put forward clear requirements for the custody and segregation of customer crypto assets in the Crypto Asset Market Regulation (MiCA). The MiCA stipulates that authorized cryptocurrency service providers (CASPs) must legally and operationally segregate customer assets from their own assets when holding crypto assets on behalf of customers[3]. Specifically, CASPs should clearly identify customer assets on the distributed ledger and ensure that the records on the ledger distinguish the tokens held by customers from the company's own tokens[3]. MiCA further emphasizes that customer assets are legally independent of CASP's property, and even if the platform goes bankrupt and is liquidated, its creditors have no right to claim against the crypto assets held in custody by customers[25]. In other words, customers have ownership or beneficial rights to their custodial assets, which do not become general claims due to the platform's bankruptcy. This provision is consistent with the existing investor asset segregation principle in the EU securities sector. Articles 67 and 68 of the official MiCA text (Regulation (EU) 2023/1114) published in June 2023 detail CASP's custody obligations and asset segregation measures, including separating customer assets from its own assets at the technical and operational levels, and establishing corresponding internal control and audit mechanisms to ensure effective segregation[26][27]. The EU's move aims to learn from past crypto platform bankruptcies, eliminate legal uncertainty regarding the ownership of customer assets, and provide investors with bankruptcy segregation protection similar to that in traditional finance. At the federal level in the United States, there are currently no uniform legal provisions specifically for cryptocurrency custody, but some states and regulatory agencies have begun to take action. Among them, the New York State Department of Financial Services (NYDFS), known for its strict regulations, issued a guidance statement in January 2023 for institutions holding cryptocurrency custody licenses (i.e., companies holding New York BitLicenses or trust licenses). The NYDFS explicitly requires that cryptocurrency custodians should "separately account for and segregate clients' virtual currencies from their own virtual currencies." This can be achieved by creating separate wallets for each client, using internal ledgers for separate accounts, or placing all client assets in a master account strictly separated from their own assets. Regardless of the method used, custodians must ensure that client assets are not included on their own balance sheet and are not used for any purpose other than secure custody. The NYDFS also reiterated that custodians must not misappropriate, lend, or divert client assets without the client's explicit instructions. This guidance is seen as a regulatory response by NYDFS to recent industry scandals such as the Celsius and FTX cases, in which courts disputed whether crypto assets held by customers belonged to customers or bankruptcy estates, resulting in losses for customers. NYDFS’s regulations establish the priority of customer assets over other claims in terms of regulation. Although the NYDFS guidance only applies to entities with business ties to New York, it is widely regarded as a benchmark for customer asset segregation in U.S. practice, given New York’s role in crypto regulation. Other states, such as Wyoming, have similar provisions in their Digital Assets Act, which confirm that digital assets held in custody are legally assets held by customers, thereby providing bankruptcy remoteness. The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) listed “proper asset custody” as one of the core principles that licensed platforms must follow in its “Guidance on Virtual Asset Trading Platform Operators” issued in 2023[5]. According to the guidelines, platforms should take measures to ensure the secure custody of customers' virtual assets, including using high-security cold wallets to store the vast majority of customer assets and setting strict withdrawal limits and multi-signature permissions for hot wallets. Furthermore, the guidelines require "separate holding of customer assets," meaning platforms must clearly distinguish customer virtual assets from their own assets. In practice, Hong Kong licensed platforms typically place customer fiat currency funds in separate trust accounts, while customer cryptocurrency assets are kept in dedicated wallet addresses or custodian accounts to achieve legal and operational segregation. For example, some platforms declare that 98% of customer assets are held in cold wallets managed by independent custodians, with only 2% used to meet daily withdrawal needs, and regularly report asset reserves to regulators. These measures aim to prevent platforms from misappropriating customer assets for self-operation or other purposes, and to reduce the risk of customer loss in extreme situations such as platform bankruptcy or hacking attacks. At the end of 2023, the SFC further issued a notice emphasizing the need for strong governance and auditing in customer asset custody, requiring senior management to regularly review custody arrangements and private key management processes, and introducing external audits to verify customer asset holdings and improve transparency. These requirements highlight the importance Hong Kong regulators place on customer asset security, striving to avoid repeating the mistakes of overseas platforms and maintain Hong Kong's reputation as a compliant crypto market through rigorous systems. Singapore's MAS, in its licensing approval process for digital payment token service providers, also examines whether applicants possess reliable custody solutions and internal controls to ensure customer assets are not misused. Although Singapore has not yet enacted specific regulations on customer asset segregation, MAS guidelines recommend that licensed institutions store customer tokens in independent on-chain addresses, separating them from operating funds, and establish daily reconciliation and asset verification mechanisms. In Japan, the Financial Services Agency (FSA) urged crypto exchanges to implement "trust-based" customer assets as early as 2018, entrusting customers' fiat currency deposits to third-party trust institutions for safekeeping, while requiring at least 95% of customers' crypto assets to be stored offline. The 2019 amendment to the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act further included crypto assets within the scope of financial products and formally stipulated that exchanges must verify customer assets daily to ensure that the amount of customer assets is not lower than the required level; if it is lower, it must be reported to the regulator immediately. This actually establishes a customer asset reserve system, which is also a special form of segregation requirement.

In summary, customer asset segregation has become a common bottom line for regulation in various jurisdictions. Whether in Europe and the United States or in the Asia-Pacific region, regulatory agencies have required crypto asset trading platforms to independently custody and manage customer assets in the form of explicit regulations or administrative guidelines, and not to treat them as company property or misappropriate them[25]. The implementation of this requirement helps protect investors from the risk of misappropriation by platform insiders or the division of assets by creditors, and enhances market confidence. However, when operating across jurisdictions, institutions still need to pay attention to the specific compliance details differences in different regions. For example, some regions require the introduction of a third-party independent custodian, while others allow platforms to keep their own assets but must meet capital or insurance requirements, etc. These differences will be discussed in later chapters. However, "not misappropriating client assets" is the absolute red line; any deviation could result in severe regulatory penalties or even criminal liability.

03 Regulatory Red Line Consensus: Combating Market Manipulation and Preventing Conflicts of Interest

This chapter explores two bottom lines related to market fairness: anti-market manipulation and conflict of interest prevention.

3.1 Anti-Market Manipulation: Market Integrity and Manipulation Prevention

The price volatility and relative lack of traditional market infrastructure in the crypto asset market make it vulnerable to manipulation, including wash trading, pump and dump, insider trading, and market manipulation. Allowing market manipulation to run rampant not only infringes on investor rights but may also damage market pricing functions and weaken public trust in the crypto market.

If market manipulation is allowed to proliferate, it will not only harm investors' rights but also undermine market pricing functions and erode public trust in the crypto market.

Therefore, regulatory agencies in major jurisdictions generally regard combating market manipulation and insider trading as a regulatory red line, requiring trading platforms and related intermediaries to take measures to monitor and prevent suspicious trading activities, and report to regulatory authorities when necessary to ensure a fair and orderly market [4]. Legally, many countries classify serious market manipulation as a criminal offense and apply the penalty standards of traditional financial markets. In the United States, the anti-manipulation laws for the securities and derivatives markets are relatively complete. For digital tokens classified as securities, Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and SEC Rule 10b-5 prohibit any market manipulation or securities fraud; insider trading is also prohibited and punished under the framework of federal securities laws and case law. For digital assets that are considered commodities (such as Bitcoin and Ethereum as recognized by US regulators), the Commodity Exchange Act (CEA) empowers the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) to govern manipulation in the derivatives market and can take enforcement action against fraudulent manipulation involving the spot market. In recent years, U.S. law enforcement agencies have actively used these laws to combat illegal activities in the cryptocurrency market. For example, in 2021, the U.S. Department of Justice prosecuted an insider trading case involving cryptocurrencies, accusing an exchange employee of profiting by buying tokens before they were listed. The SEC filed a parallel securities fraud charge, arguing that the relevant tokens were securities. This was the first case of "insider trading" enforcement in the cryptocurrency field, demonstrating that the U.S. will not relax its punishment for unfair trading based on the form of the asset. Also noteworthy is the CFTC's 2021 lawsuit against an executive of a cryptocurrency trading platform, accusing them of tacitly allowing users to engage in wash trading, thereby creating false liquidity and misleading the market, and pursuing liability under the anti-manipulation provisions of the Commodity Exchange Act. Although the U.S. does not yet have specific legislation targeting manipulation in the cryptocurrency spot market, regulatory agencies have carried out numerous enforcement actions using existing laws (securities laws, commodity laws, and anti-fraud provisions). Meanwhile, state attorneys general offices in New York and other states have also used broad anti-securities fraud powers, such as the Martin Act, to investigate trading platforms suspected of manipulation (such as the 2018 NYAG investigation report on volume manipulation at several exchanges). In general, the United States emphasizes the principle of "neutrality of application of law". Even if emerging crypto assets are manipulated in a manner similar to that of traditional securities/commodities, they may also face corresponding legal sanctions. The European Union has established a comprehensive ban on insider trading and market manipulation in the traditional securities field through the Market Abuse Regulation (MAR). However, the MAR mainly applies to financial instruments in regulated markets and does not directly cover most crypto assets. In order to fill this gap, the European Union has introduced market integrity obligations for crypto assets in the MiCA. Article 80 of the MiCA requires that no one who is engaged in crypto trading shall use inside information to trade related assets or engage in manipulation. At the same time, the MiCA requires CASPs operating trading platforms to establish market monitoring mechanisms and have the ability to identify and deal with market abuse.[4] Specific measures include: trading systems must have built-in monitoring algorithms capable of detecting abnormally large orders, frequent order cancellations, and other possible signs of manipulation; maintaining platform trading order during periods of sharp price fluctuations to prevent market manipulation; setting thresholds for price increases or decreases or trading volume to automatically reject orders exceeding reasonable limits; and promptly reporting any suspected manipulation or insider trading to the relevant authorities. Furthermore, MiCA requires platforms to continuously disclose buy and sell quotes and market depth information, as well as timely disclosure of transaction data, to improve market transparency and reduce opportunities for opaque operations. These regulations essentially draw on the EU's experience in the securities market, applying many principles of "same business, same risk, same rules" to cryptocurrency trading. In 2025, the EU, in its draft of a new Anti-Money Laundering Regulation (AMLR), proposed banning anonymous transactions and privacy coins (see below), which is also part of preventing market manipulation (improving traceability). In terms of enforcement, with MiCA taking effect, securities regulators in member states (such as the French AMF and German BaFin) will have clear authorization to investigate and prosecute manipulation in the cryptocurrency market. For example, if someone incites a group in a Telegram group to collectively buy a certain token to drive up its price and then sell it for profit, they may be found guilty of violating the market manipulation ban and be punished. This demonstrates that the EU is gradually extending its market abuse regulation to the crypto space, achieving regulatory extension and fairness. The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC), when issuing licenses to virtual asset trading platforms, requires licensed platforms to establish market monitoring departments or systems to monitor abnormal transactions in real time. According to SFC guidelines, platforms should promptly identify and prevent transactions attempting to manipulate the market, such as wash trading through related accounts, false order declarations and cancellations, and retain logs for regulatory inquiries. If significant suspicious manipulation is discovered, the platform must report to the SFC and law enforcement agencies. Although Hong Kong currently does not include crypto assets under the market manipulation offenses under the Securities and Futures Ordinance (because most crypto assets are not defined as securities), licensed platforms still have a compliance obligation to ensure market fairness. This is a practice of "implementing regulatory objectives through licensing conditions." In Singapore, the Securities and Futures Act (SFA) applies the market manipulation and insider trading provisions of the Securities and Futures Act (SFA) to digital tokens listed on regulated markets (such as authorized exchanges) if they are classified as securities or derivatives. However, for pure crypto asset trading not included in the definition of financial instruments, Singapore currently primarily relies on industry guidelines requiring traders to conduct self-regulatory monitoring. Nevertheless, the MAS has repeatedly warned of money laundering and manipulation risks in the crypto market, urging investors to be vigilant. This year (2025), Singapore is also considering amending relevant laws to include certain token trading activities involving public interest under the Financial Markets Conduct Act, such as prohibiting the dissemination of false or misleading information that affects token prices, to address the lack of sufficient enforcement basis. It is worth noting that to assist regulators and platforms in fulfilling their monitoring obligations, some professional blockchain analytics companies and market regulatory technology solutions are beginning to be applied to anti-manipulation. For example, some trading platforms use on-chain analytics tools to monitor the circulation of funds between multiple accounts to determine whether there is "multi-account collusion for manipulation"; some institutions have also developed AI models that can identify abnormal price patterns and order book behavior. Regulators are increasingly relying on such technologies to improve monitoring efficiency. For example, the US SEC has established a dedicated crypto asset monitoring team, using big data analysis of exchange trading records to detect abnormal fluctuations and investigate whether there is manipulation behind them. The EU's ESMA and regulators in various countries are also exploring the establishment of a cross-platform crypto trading reporting database to identify cross-market manipulation. In summary, combating market manipulation and insider trading is a common regulatory red line across jurisdictions, with differences only in the specific implementation methods due to variations in regulatory scope and legal authorization. Looking at the trend, as the crypto market deepens its integration with traditional finance, countries will increasingly tend to incorporate crypto trading activities into existing market regulatory frameworks to achieve equal constraints. For example, the Financial Stability Board (FSB) recommendations in 2023 emphasized that countries should ensure "effective regulation and supervision of the crypto asset market to maintain market integrity," including equipping sufficient enforcement tools to curb manipulation and fraud. This global guiding principle is expected to translate into clearer regulatory requirements in various jurisdictions, ensuring that manipulating the crypto market will be subject to legal accountability, whether in New York, London, or Singapore. For market participants, this means creating a more fair and transparent trading environment, which is also a necessary condition for the long-term healthy development of the industry. 3.2 Conflict of Interest Prevention: Business Separation and Internal Governance Conflict of interest prevention is a fundamental requirement to ensure that financial institutions fulfill their fiduciary responsibilities and protect the interests of their clients. In the field of crypto asset trading, potential conflicts of interest include: trading platforms acting as both market operators and engaging in proprietary trading or controlling related market makers, potentially profiting from client order information; platforms issuing their own tokens and listing them for trading, leading to price maintenance and information asymmetry issues; and executives or employees possessing sensitive market information and engaging in personal trading (insider trading). If left unregulated, these conflicts of interest will harm client interests and market fairness, and may even trigger systemic risks (as seen in the collapse of some exchanges due to affiliated companies engaging in high-risk trading and misappropriating client assets). Therefore, regulatory agencies in various countries consider preventing and managing conflicts of interest as one of the red lines, requiring crypto asset service providers to establish internal controls and institutional arrangements to identify, mitigate, and disclose potential conflicts. The MiCA sets forth clear and mandatory requirements for conflict of interest management for CASPs. According to MiCA Section 72[28], crypto asset service providers must develop and maintain effective policies and procedures to identify, prevent, manage and disclose potential conflicts of interest. These conflicts may occur between: (a) the service provider and its shareholders, directors or employees; (b) different clients; or (c) when the service provider and its affiliates are conducting multiple business functions. The MiCA requires service providers to assess and update their conflict of interest policies at least annually and to take all appropriate measures to resolve any conflicts. At the same time, service providers must disclose the nature, source and mitigation steps of general conflicts of interest in a prominent position on their website so that clients are informed. For CASPs operating trading platforms, the MiCA further stipulates that there should be particularly comprehensive procedures to avoid conflicts of interest between themselves and their clients in trading, including preventing transactions with proprietary counterparties on the matching system and restricting platform personnel from trading using undisclosed information. MiCA also authorizes regulators to refine disclosure formats and other technical standards, demonstrating that the EU considers conflicts of interest a key area requiring strong regulatory intervention. One of the considerations behind MiCA is to learn from the risks arising from proprietary trading and related-party transactions by some exchanges in the past, ensuring "fair play": platforms cannot operate as casinos while simultaneously placing bets themselves, thus deceiving other gamblers. It's worth noting that MiCA not only requires service providers to manage conflicts of interest themselves, but also has similar provisions for asset reference stablecoin issuers (such as requiring disclosure of potential conflicts of interest arising from managing reserve assets, reflecting the EU's unified requirements for all types of entities to establish firewalls against conflicts of interest). Traditional US financial markets have long had systems in place to address conflicts of interest (such as the separation of proprietary trading from exchanges and the firewall regulations for banks). Regarding the crypto sector, there are currently no specific regulations mandating that trading platforms split their businesses or prohibit proprietary trading, but regulatory officials have repeatedly expressed concerns about the risks of "vertical integration." For example, a commissioner of the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) stated in a 2024 public statement that the integration of multiple roles such as exchange, broker, market maker, and custodian, as seen in FTX, coupled with a lack of external oversight, has led to significant conflicts of interest and risks. Regulators should develop rules to restrict such vertically integrated structures. The statement mentioned that the FTX collapse "highlighted the serious harm caused by the lack of regulatory oversight of conflicts of interest." Despite FTX's collapse... While not fully regulated in the US at the time, its collapse prompted US lawmakers and regulators to reconsider whether regulations similar to the "separation of trading and advisory services" in the Securities Act were needed for crypto exchanges. Currently, some US Congressional proposals (such as the 2022 draft Digital Goods Consumer Protection Act) have considered prohibiting crypto exchanges from engaging in activities that conflict with customer interests, such as prohibiting exchanges from lending customer assets or restricting their affiliates from participating in platform trading; however, these bills have not yet passed. On the other hand, US regulators have advocated for conflict of interest prevention through enforcement. For example, the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), in its warnings to platforms like Coinbase, mentioned that allowing executives to sell tokens prematurely and the platform itself investing in and listing token projects could constitute a conflict of interest and harm investors, requiring full disclosure and control. Furthermore, the US Department of Justice's prosecution of certain practitioners for allegedly profiting from trading using undisclosed token listing information also falls under the category of punishing insider conflicts. In addition, at the licensing level, the New York State Department of Financial Services requires BitLicenses... Licensed companies are submitting conflict-of-interest policies, outlining restrictions on personal transactions by directors and senior executives, as well as mitigation measures for potential conflicts of interest arising from multiple business operations. These measures indicate that while the US lacks a comprehensive MiCA-style framework, enforcement and regulatory actions are increasingly prioritizing conflict of interest compliance for crypto platforms. The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) explicitly requires licensed platforms to avoid conflicts of interest in its "Guidelines for Virtual Asset Trading Platforms." Specific measures include: platforms are prohibited from engaging in any form of proprietary trading for their own accounts (i.e., not acting as "market makers"); if a subsidiary within the platform group engages in market-making activities, it must report to the SFC and ensure a strict information firewall (Chinese Wall) to prevent insider information leaks; personal crypto transactions by platform executives and employees are also restricted, requiring declaration and internal compliance approval. Furthermore, if a platform intends to list tokens with which it has a vested interest (such as tokens of projects invested in by the platform or native tokens issued by the platform), the SFC must... Full disclosure is required, and listing may be denied on a case-by-case basis to prevent platforms from "listing on one hand and profiting on the other." This practice in Hong Kong is consistent with its securities market regulatory practices, such as profit sharing between brokerage firms and proprietary trading, and avoiding conflicts between agency trading and proprietary trading. After the SFC issued licenses in 2023, the first batch of licensed platforms in Hong Kong declared in their public information that they did not engage in proprietary trading and would not compete with clients for profits, in order to gain investors' trust. This created a red line in the system: the platform only acts as an intermediary, not a market counterparty, thus reducing conflicts of interest. International organizations are also concerned about this issue. The Financial Stability Board (FSB) in July 2023... The high-level recommendations on crypto regulation released in May clearly stated that each jurisdiction should "ensure that crypto asset service providers that combine multiple functions are subject to appropriate regulatory oversight, including requirements for conflicts of interest and separation of certain functions"[26]. This is equivalent to calling on countries at the global level to regulate the business models of crypto trading platforms and, if necessary, to force the separation of certain conflicting functions (such as the separation of trading and custody, and the separation of brokerage and market making) to prevent one institution from being both player and referee. The FSB's position is supported by institutions such as IOSCO: IOSCO also suggested in its 2022 consultation report that regulators should require crypto exchanges to disclose proprietary trading, restrict improper trading by employees, and may draw on the experience of structural segmented regulation in traditional finance to reduce conflicts of interest. It is foreseeable that, driven by the FSB and IOSCO, "the same risk, the same function, the same rules" will be gradually implemented in the field of conflict of interest management, and a unified international standard may emerge in the near future. In short, conflict of interest prevention has been incorporated into the basic requirements of various countries for the regulation of the crypto asset industry. From the EU MiCA From mandatory rules in Hong Kong and Singapore to licensing requirements, and from statements and enforcement by US regulators, all these send a clear signal: trading platforms and other intermediaries must establish sound internal control mechanisms to prevent the exploitation of clients' unfavorable information for personal gain, and any such behavior must be promptly disclosed and stopped. The establishment of this red line will help restore market confidence damaged by several scandals and promote the industry towards greater transparency and integrity. For operating institutions, this requires investing more resources in internal governance, such as introducing independent compliance officers to oversee trading, conducting regular conflict-of-interest risk assessments, and training employees on ethical standards, in order to meet the expectations of regulators in various regions and maintain their reputation. Stablecoins, as crypto tokens pegged to fiat currencies or other assets, have developed rapidly globally, attracting significant attention from regulatory bodies worldwide. On the one hand, stablecoins promise to improve payment efficiency and financial inclusion; on the other hand, their widespread use could impact financial stability and monetary sovereignty, especially when stablecoin issuance lacks sufficient reserves or transparency, posing a greater risk of collapse (such as the collapse of the algorithmic stablecoin UST in 2022). Therefore, countries are exploring regulatory pathways for stablecoins. However, due to differences in legislative philosophies and financial systems, stablecoin regulation has become one of the most contentious issues across jurisdictions. The main disagreements lie in: the issuing authority, reserve and capital requirements, investor protection measures, and restrictions on transaction usage. 4.1 Licensing and Limits of Fiat Currency Stablecoins The EU, in its MiCA... China classifies stablecoins into two categories: Electronic Money Tokens (EMTs), which are stablecoins pegged to a single fiat currency; and Asset Reference Tokens (ARTs), which are stablecoins pegged to a basket of assets or fiat currency. MiCA imposes strict entry and regulation on both categories. For EMTs, issuers must obtain a license from a credit institution (bank) or electronic money institution and issue them with the approval of the regulatory body. Issuers must hold highly liquid reserve assets (mainly corresponding fiat currency deposits or high-quality government bonds) equal to the value of the issued tokens to ensure 1:1 solvency. MiCA also prohibits paying interest to EMT holders to prevent competition with deposits. Most notably, to prevent stablecoins from outside the Eurozone from impacting monetary policy, MiCA introduced a transaction usage cap: for non-Euro-pegged stablecoins (such as USDT pegged to the US dollar), their daily trading volume cannot exceed €200 million or 1 million transactions; otherwise, issuers must take measures to restrict usage (suspending issuance or redemption if necessary). This "€200 million/day" and "1 million transactions/day" limit is significant. The dual threshold of "day" aims to prevent any single stablecoin from becoming excessively popular and replacing the euro for payments. This is a unique preventative regulatory measure in the EU, attracting significant attention from the industry (known as the "hard cap" for stablecoin trading). Furthermore, MiCA imposes higher requirements on issuers of Significant EMT/ART stablecoins, including more frequent reporting, stricter liquidity management, and reserve custody rules. From 2024, MiCA regulations on stablecoins will take effect first, and issuers must achieve full compliance after the transition period; otherwise, they will be prohibited from providing stablecoin-related services within the EU. This EU framework is arguably the most comprehensive and stringent stablecoin regulation globally, benchmarking against the regulatory standards for electronic money institutions and banks issuing payment instruments in traditional finance. In contrast to the EU, the US currently lacks specific federal-level regulations for stablecoins, creating a significant regulatory vacuum. In recent years, the US Congress has published numerous reports and draft bills regarding stablecoins. The President's Working Group on Financial Markets (PWG) in 2021... The report recommended that stablecoin issuance be restricted to regulated depository institutions (such as banks) and called on Congress to legislate. Subsequently, several proposals, including the "Stablecoin Transparency and Protection Act" and the "Digital Commodity Stablecoin Act," were drafted, but none passed due to differing political views. As a result, under current law, stablecoin issuers can only operate within the existing framework: some companies regulated by trust bylaws (such as Paxos and Circle, which use state trust licenses) issue stablecoins and are subject to regulation by state financial regulatory authorities; USDT issued by other unlicensed foreign companies (such as Tether) operates outside of US regulation, only partially constrained by US regulations due to their bank custody accounts. Federal regulators can only exert indirect pressure; for example, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) allowed national banks to issue stablecoins in 2021 with prudential conditions, and the Federal Reserve and the FDIC warned banks to be cautious about participating in stablecoin reserve operations. At the state level, the New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS) was the first to implement reserve and audit requirements for stablecoins under its regulation (such as BUSD and USDP, which are NYDFS-approved). In 2022, the NYDFS... The guidelines issued in New York require that dollar-denominated stablecoins issued in New York be 100% held in cash or short-term U.S. Treasury bonds, with daily redemptions and no interest. Wyoming has passed innovative laws allowing Reserve Banks for Digital Assets (SPDIs) and newly established stablecoin institutions to issue stablecoins, but there are no successful cases yet. In the absence of unified rules, the U.S. stablecoin market is highly fragmented: large players like Tether and Circle maintain high reserves and regularly disclose asset proofs, but loopholes still exist (such as the TerraUSD algorithmic coin, which grew rapidly in a regulatory vacuum and ultimately collapsed). This regulatory vacuum has alerted U.S. legislators, and in the second half of 2023, Congress again attempted to push for the Stablecoin Regulatory Act, trying to establish a federal licensing system and grant the Federal Reserve oversight of non-bank stablecoin issuance. However, the legislative process remains uncertain. Against this backdrop, U.S. authorities are temporarily managing risks through enforcement: for example, the SEC has issued warnings about certain stablecoins suspected of being securities (such as a dollar-denominated stablecoin issued by a social media platform), and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has taken similar measures. The United States has adopted a relatively conservative approach to stablecoin regulation. In June 2022, the Japanese Diet passed amendments to laws such as the Payment Services Act, clarifying the legal status and issuance qualifications of stablecoins for the first time. The new law classifies stablecoins as “electronic payment instruments” and requires that they can only be issued by regulated legal institutions, including registered banks in Japan, restricted remittance operators (which must have high capital) or trust companies[6]. This provision excludes general private enterprises, meaning that entities like Tether cannot legally issue stablecoins in Japan. The new law also stipulates that eligible stablecoins must be pegged to the Japanese yen or other fiat currencies and can be redeemed by holders at face value. At the same time, it prohibits the issuance of non-guaranteed forms such as algorithmic stablecoins. For reserves, 100% fiat currency margin deposits are required to be held by regulated institutions. Furthermore, Japan's Financial Services Agency (FSA) is very cautious about foreign stablecoins entering the Japanese market. Currently, Japanese exchanges generally do not list major overseas stablecoins (such as USDT), while JPY-pegged coins issued by trust companies (such as "Progmat Coin" issued by the Mitsubishi UFJ Trust) are under testing. In 2024, Japan discussed further restrictions: for example, the FSA proposed prohibiting ordinary banks from directly issuing stablecoins on public blockchains (considering the risk too high, only trust banks and the like can issue them), and requiring the full application of KYC/travel rules for stablecoin transfers. Under the Japanese model, stablecoins are more like an alternative to bank deposits, strictly regulated by banking laws and payment regulations, reflecting a high degree of emphasis on financial stability and consumer protection. The advantage of this model is high security, but the disadvantage is that it inhibits the innovation of non-banks. Currently, there is no large-scale circulation of stablecoins in Japan, but if major banks plan to issue yen stablecoins, they will operate entirely within the regulatory purview, and the risks will be relatively controllable. In 2017, a discussion document was released, which explicitly stated that algorithmic stablecoins were not allowed and that the focus of regulation would be on payment-type stablecoins referenced by fiat currency. In 2023, the Stablecoin Ordinance (draft name) was completed and enacted in August 2025[7]. The ordinance requires that issuers of any stablecoins pegged to fiat currency issued or circulated in Hong Kong must obtain a license from the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA). The license application must meet strict conditions, including establishing an entity in Hong Kong, having a certain amount of legal capital, implementing risk management and technical audits, etc. The ordinance requires issuers to hold 100% reserve assets (limited to highly liquid assets) and redeem stablecoins at face value when holders raise them. At the same time, the HKMA is given supervisory and inspection powers to review reserve status and operations. The special feature of Hong Kong regulation is that it does not limit banks to issuance, but ensures that any issuer is subject to prudential supervision similar to that of banks. This may leave some room for non-bank financial technology companies to issue stablecoins, but the regulatory costs are also not low. Hong Kong plans to 2024-25 The first batch of licenses was approved in 2018, emphasizing a small-scale initial issuance and steady progress. Notably, Hong Kong's regulations include stablecoins within the scope of anti-money laundering ordinances, requiring issuers and distributors to implement KYC/AML obligations. This move by Hong Kong signifies the establishment of a stablecoin regulatory benchmark in another Asian financial center, unlike Singapore, which primarily relies on guidance rather than legislation. For the market, Hong Kong's framework provides a compliant path for issuing and operating stablecoins, potentially attracting licensed stablecoin issuers to apply. 4.2 Capital and Reserve Requirements for Non-Fiat Asset-Backed Stablecoins (Asset-Pegged Tokens) Besides stablecoins pegged to a single fiat currency, some stablecoins are backed by a basket of fiat currencies, commodities, or crypto assets (such as the early-envisioned Libra, which is pegged to multiple reserve assets), or maintain their peg through algorithmic collateralization. These stablecoins, known as "Asset Reference Tokens" (ARTs), have more complex value stabilization mechanisms and higher risks. Regulators in various countries have different requirements for ARTs. They tend to take a more cautious approach. Especially after the Libra project (later renamed Diem) triggered a global regulatory backlash in 2019, most jurisdictions explicitly stated that such cross-border basket currencies could threaten financial sovereignty and stability and should be strictly regulated. The EU's MiCA (Mutual Regulatory Authority) includes Asset Reference Tokens (ARTs) within its regulatory scope, requiring issuers to obtain licenses and comply with a series of requirements similar to EMTs, including white paper disclosure, reserve custody, capital, and liquidity plans. However, given that ARTs are not pegged to a single fiat currency, MiCA's regulation is more stringent: First, ART issuers must hold a relatively higher minimum capital (at least €350,000 or 2% of the value of reserves), higher than the requirements for EMT issuers. Simultaneously, ART issuers must establish a clear reserve asset custody policy to ensure that reserve assets are completely segregated from the issuer's own assets and cannot be misappropriated. MiCA stipulates that reserve assets can be held by qualified custodians (such as licensed CASP custodians or banks) and requires highly diversified reserves to reduce correlation risk. Furthermore, ARTs... Issuers must establish a regular audit mechanism, disclosing reserve composition and audit reports quarterly to enhance transparency. For significant ARTs, MiCA authorizes regulators to impose additional requirements, such as limiting business size or requiring more detailed risk analysis. The EU also prohibits issuers from providing any additional profit incentives to ART holders under MiCA, aiming to prevent ARTs from becoming disguised investment products. Overall, MiCA requirements for ARTs cover governance, risk management, and user protection, hoping to bring their risks to a manageable level. Following the Libra incident, the US exerted pressure on ARTs through a multi-agency effort, forcing the project to modify its design or even terminate. Although no specific law has been enacted, it can be speculated that if a Libra-like ART were to emerge, the US might invoke Title I of the Dodd-Frank Act (Systemically Important Payment Instruments) or securities laws to regulate it. For example, if an ART involves a basket of securities as reserves, the SEC might classify it as an ETF fund share, requiring registration; if it involves payment functions and is large in scale, the Financial Stability Oversight Council (FSOC) might intervene. The issuer may be classified as a systemically important institution and subject to Federal Reserve regulation. Currently, there are no large-scale ART (Automatic Traditional Coin) stablecoins operating in the US market (most mainstream stablecoins are single-currency pegged), so regulatory disagreements mainly stem from differing stances. Federal Reserve officials have stated that multi-currency stablecoins may require special review by the central bank. Some US think tanks have suggested classifying such stablecoins as "shadow banking currencies," requiring their issuers to adhere to strict asset portfolio restrictions and reserve requirements similar to money market funds. However, due to a lack of practical examples, the US has not established a fixed path for this. In terms of enforcement, if ART leads to investment losses, regulators may invoke commodity or securities laws. For example, the algorithmic stablecoin Ampleforth was investigated by the SEC for potentially selling securities through an ICO; its algorithmic nature (pegging to a basket of assets or an algorithmic formula) is not exempt from securities laws. These examples reflect the "principle-driven" nature of US regulation: there is no specific law, but relevant laws are applied on a case-by-case basis. In 2022, Singapore consulted the public on stablecoins, proposing rules for single-currency stablecoins (such as requiring the primary reference currency to be...). Stablecoins typically have G10 currencies and minimum reserve asset quality requirements, but tend to discourage multi-asset-backed stablecoins for the time being. The MAS's approach is to first regulate single-currency stablecoins and then decide whether to allow more complex structures based on international consensus. Hong Kong's current regulations primarily target fiat-pegged coins, directly refusing to permit non-fiat-pegged tokens (such as algorithmic stablecoins and commodity-backed coins). In 2023, the South Korean Financial Services Commission declared that exchange trading of algorithmic stablecoins was prohibited and held a negative attitude towards any stablecoins with yields or complex mechanisms. Japan, which only allows fiat-pegged stablecoins, has no room for ART (Automatic Stablecoin) stablecoins. In summary, there are significant differences in stablecoin regulation between Europe and the US: the EU has already adopted MiCA (Missing Coin). A comprehensive set of regulations has been established, especially regarding explicit requirements for international basket currencies; the United States, however, is still discussing and exploring, focusing more on legislation for single-currency stablecoins in the short term. Most major Asian economies are cautious and adopt a tighter approach. This difference means that companies engaged in stablecoin businesses must address drastically different compliance requirements when expanding globally. Operating in the EU market requires obtaining a license and monitoring daily transaction volumes to ensure they do not exceed thresholds; in the US market, while there are no explicit licensing requirements, there are uncertain regulatory risks and potential enforcement; in regions like Japan and Hong Kong, they may face direct licensing restrictions or bans. Therefore, regulatory differences are particularly evident in this area and have become a key challenge for future international regulatory coordination. This chapter discusses three other issues where significant cross-jurisdictional regulatory differences exist: regulation of crypto derivatives trading, the legality of privacy coins, and regulatory exploration of Real Asset Tokenization (RWA) and decentralized finance (DeFi). Due to issues involving investor protection, criminal enforcement, technological anonymity, and the boundaries of financial innovation, different countries have adopted different strategies, and a unified international standard has not yet been established. 5.1 Market Access and Investor Protection for Crypto Derivatives Crypto derivatives refer to futures, options, and contracts for difference (CFDs) based on the price of crypto assets. These are contract products. Such products can be used for hedging and speculation, amplifying both returns and risks. High leverage and high volatility make them extremely dangerous for ordinary investors. Traditional financial markets have strict entry requirements for derivatives trading (exchange and clearinghouse licenses, etc.), while in recent years, a large number of crypto derivatives platforms have operated unlicensed overseas, attracting global users. Regulatory agencies in different countries have varying attitudes towards this: some allow and regulate it, some restrict retail participation, and some completely prohibit it. This is one of the most direct manifestations of the differences in crypto market regulation. The United States classifies Bitcoin, Ethereum, etc., as commodities; therefore, their futures and options are commodity derivatives, governed by the Commodity Exchange Act (CEA). The CFTC is the primary regulator, requiring any facility offering crypto derivatives trading to Americans to register with the CFTC as a futures exchange (DCM) or swap execution facility (SEF), and brokers to register as futures commission merchants (FCMs), etc. At the same time, platforms and intermediaries must comply with regulations regarding client asset protection and transaction reporting. To date, the United States has approved a small number of compliant exchanges to offer crypto futures (such as the Chicago Mercantile Exchange CME). Bitcoin futures were launched in 2017, but these markets primarily cater to institutional and professional investors, with relatively limited trading volumes. The vast majority of retail investors prefer to access unregistered platforms (such as BitMEX and Binance in the past), which violates US law. The CFTC has been rigorously enforcing regulations against such platforms for several years: in 2021, the CFTC and FinCEN jointly penalized BitMEX for illegally providing trading services to the US and violating AML rules; in 2023, the CFTC sued Binance and its CEO, accusing them of circumventing US regulations for years, tacitly allowing US users to trade highly leveraged derivatives, and failing to fulfill their obligations to verify customer identities and monitor manipulation. These actions demonstrate that the US uses "offshore platform enforcement" as a crucial means of protecting its investors. It's worth noting that the US also restricts retail participation in highly complex derivatives: for example, the SEC prohibits retail investors from purchasing contracts for difference (CFDs) related to crypto assets or certain over-the-counter derivatives, and the CFTC... Complex swaps were not promoted to retail. In general, the US model allows crypto derivatives, but transactions must be conducted within the regulatory framework, and any attempt to circumvent the regulations will be severely punished. This model is consistent with the US approach to other financial derivatives ("no license is no law"). However, the SEC may also intervene in derivatives not included in the scope of commodities (such as options based on security tokens). For example, the SEC has warned that some platforms offering swap contracts based on unregistered security tokens are suspected of being illegal. It can be seen that the US assigns different regulatory jurisdictions according to the attributes of the underlying asset, but the core is that all derivatives require licenses and there is no room for regulatory arbitrage. After assessing the risks of crypto derivatives to consumers, the UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) announced in October 2020 a ban on the sale and distribution of any derivatives and ETNs referencing unregulated crypto assets to retail customers[8][9]. The ban took effect in January 2021 and covers contracts for difference (CFDs), futures, options and exchange-traded notes (ETNs). This move was the first of its kind globally at the time: UK regulators believed that crypto assets were unsuitable for retail investors due to their unreliable valuation, frequent market manipulation, high volatility, and lack of consumer understanding. The ban was expected to save UK retail investors approximately £53 million annually. Under the ban, UK retail customers were prohibited from obtaining any exposure to crypto derivatives through UK companies. The FCA warned that any company offering such services to UK retail investors was breaking the law and would be treated as a scam. However, in October 2025, the FCA, citing the need to enhance the competitiveness of the financial center, announced the lifting of the retail ban on crypto asset ETNs, but maintained the ban on derivatives such as CFDs. This means that regulated venues in the UK are now allowed to sell some approved crypto ETNs. Retail investors are allowed access, but highly leveraged derivatives remain prohibited. This policy change reflects the UK's attempt to balance investor protection with innovative competitiveness. Currently, UK retail investors wishing to trade Bitcoin futures and options cannot do so through UK-based platforms and must rely on overseas channels. The UK's strong stance on derivatives is prominent among major economies, leading some active traders to migrate to Europe or other markets. Looking ahead, the UK may decide whether to adjust its strategy after the effects of the EU's MiCA become clearer. However, in the short term, protecting retail investors from losses on complex, high-risk derivatives remains a priority for UK regulation. In the EU, MiCA itself does not directly regulate derivatives, as it targets the spot market and issuance. Crypto derivatives, if physically settled and the underlying asset is not a financial instrument, may theoretically fall outside the scope of current financial directives. However, many member states have already recognized crypto derivatives as financial instruments. For example, Germany's BaFin classifies crypto CFDs and futures as financial derivatives under the Financial Instruments Directive II (MiFID II) and requires licensing. France's AMF... It also requires any platform offering crypto derivatives trading to obtain the corresponding investment service provider qualification. In 2018, ESMA included crypto CFDs in its product intervention measures for contracts for difference (CFDs), setting a leverage cap of 2:1 (lower than the 30:1 for forex) to protect investors. This indicates that the EU as a whole tends to allow regulated offerings but strengthen risk management. Individual member states have considered stricter measures; for example, Belgium briefly banned the distribution of crypto derivatives to retail investors. Currently, there is no unified ban at the EU level like the UK's; the MiFID framework and national regulatory practices effectively provide a path to derivatives compliance. Therefore, EU retail investors can trade limited-leverage crypto CFDs through licensed brokers in Cyprus, or cash-settled Bitcoin ETNs through the European Futures Exchange (Eurex). It is expected that with the implementation of MiCA and updates to EU financial regulations, unified regulations specifically for crypto derivatives may be introduced (ESMA is already studying this). However, as things stand, the EU maintains a cautiously open attitude: it is permissible under regulation, but abuse and excessive leverage must be prevented, and investors must be given sufficient risk warnings. Asian countries exhibit drastically different approaches to regulating crypto derivatives. Japan revised its Financial Instruments and Exchange Act in 2019, including crypto asset derivatives in the category of financial instruments and imposing registration regulations on businesses engaged in crypto spread/futures trading. Japanese exchanges can offer crypto margin trading to clients, but Japanese self-regulatory rules have significantly reduced the maximum leverage ratio from 25:1 to 2:1. Therefore, Japanese users can still trade low-leverage Bitcoin contracts, but the market size is limited. South Korea has outright banned its exchanges from offering any form of crypto futures or options, deeming the risk too high for retail investors. South Korean investors can only trade through overseas platforms, but the South Korean government has strengthened its monitoring in recent years, requiring banks to restrict remittances to overseas crypto derivatives platforms. When Hong Kong introduced its licensing system in 2023, it explicitly prohibited offering crypto derivatives trading to retail investors, allowing only spot trading of the underlying assets. Professional investors' participation in derivatives through Hong Kong platforms is also strictly limited. Hong Kong only allows asset management companies approved by the Securities and Futures Commission to issue crypto-linked structured products or ETFs. Trading in regulated markets (such as cryptocurrency futures ETFs listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, open to the public). Singapore's MAS takes a cautious approach to derivatives. In 2019, it approved the listing of Bitcoin and Ethereum swap contracts on the Singapore Exchange (SGX), but stipulated that retail investors could not participate directly, opening the market only to institutional and professional investors. Furthermore, in its enhanced investor protection guidelines in 2022, the MAS restricted retail access to high-risk crypto products, including derivatives, and required local service providers not to easily promote any crypto trading services to the public through ATMs or advertisements. Overall, most major Asian markets restrict retail access to crypto derivatives: Japan allows it but with strict regulations and low leverage; Hong Kong, Singapore, and South Korea almost completely prohibit retail participation, opening limited channels only to institutions/professionals. This contrasts with the openness of some Western markets. The direct impact of regulatory disagreements surrounding crypto derivatives is "regulatory arbitrage" in global market liquidity. In regions with strict regulations or bans (such as the US, UK, and Hong Kong), a large number of users still use non-compliant platforms through VPNs and overseas accounts, creating enforcement challenges. Consequently, trading volume is concentrated on a few large platforms in regions with weak regulation, which increases the accumulation of risks. International regulators have recognized this problem. In 2023, when the Financial Stability Board (FSB) released its recommendations on a global crypto regulatory framework, it emphasized the need to strengthen cross-border enforcement cooperation and called on countries to jointly curb unlicensed crypto derivatives activities. In addition, IOSCO in 2023... In 2018, policy recommendations regarding cryptocurrency trading platforms were released, including regulatory standards for derivatives businesses, such as the requirement for platforms to disclose leverage settings and liquidation mechanisms. It is foreseeable that the global landscape will tend to narrow differences in the future: a ban on high leverage for retail investors may become a consensus, while professional markets will develop steadily through licensing. The UK's recent lifting of the ETN ban indicates that a moderate opening of vetted products may become a trend in balancing protection and development. The EU may not ban it outright like the UK, but rather strengthen investor protection requirements within systems such as MiFID, such as mandatory negative balance protection and standardized risk warnings. Some regions in Asia may adjust at the appropriate time; for example, if Hong Kong verifies the safety of professional markets, a limited opening of retail participation in approved derivatives (with accompanying education) is not ruled out in the future. In short, the regulation of cryptocurrency derivatives is moving from divergence to convergence, based on international regulatory standards frameworks, with various jurisdictions gradually tightening their crackdown on illegal platforms and balancing market demand within compliant channels.

5.2 The Legal Status and Regulatory Challenges of Privacy Coins

Privacy coins (Privacy Coins)

5.2 The Legal Status and Regulatory Challenges of Privacy Coins

Privacy coins (Privacy Coins)

... Privacy coins refer to cryptocurrencies that achieve transaction anonymity and address concealment through technical means (such as ring signatures, zero-knowledge proofs, etc.), such as Monero (XMR), Zcash (ZEC), and Dash (DASH). Supporters believe privacy coins protect financial privacy rights, but regulators worry that they could be widely used for money laundering, evading sanctions, and other illegal activities, as traditional on-chain analysis is almost ineffective in tracking transaction flows. Regulatory policies regarding privacy coins vary significantly across countries: some explicitly prohibit them, some strictly restrict their circulation, and some have not yet taken direct action but are indirectly combating them through AML rules. Japan was one of the first countries to ban privacy coin trading. In 2018, influenced by the Coincheck exchange hack (where hackers used anonymous coins to launder assets), the Japanese Financial Services Agency (FSA) required domestic registered exchanges to delist all highly anonymous cryptocurrencies, including Monero and Dash. This requirement was implemented through regulations of the self-regulatory organization (Japan Crypto Asset Exchange Association JVCEA), and newly applied exchange licenses are also prohibited from listing anonymous coins. Officials have made it clear that anonymous coins violate the "traceability" principle of Japan's anti-money laundering system and should not exist in the compliant market. Similarly, after the revision of the Specific Financial Information Act in March 2021, the Financial Services Commission (FSC) of South Korea issued guidelines prohibiting virtual asset service providers from processing "anonymous digital assets whose transaction records cannot be identified," and various South Korean exchanges subsequently delisted privacy coins[10]. South Korea also prohibits hiding the source of transactions through coin mixing services. Dubai and other Middle Eastern regions have followed suit: Dubai, UAE's 2023 Virtual Asset Regulation prohibits the issuance or trading of cryptocurrencies with enhanced anonymity. These jurisdictions have adopted a strategy of direct banning, believing that such assets do more harm than good and should not be allowed. The EU has not yet completely banned privacy coins, but is moving in that direction. The political agreement reached between the European Parliament and the Council on the Anti-Money Laundering Regulation (AMLR) in 2023 proposed that from 2027, crypto service providers should be prohibited from trading with anonymous wallets or anonymous coins[12]. According to the agreement reached on May 9, 2025, the EU will ban privacy coins. According to information released recently, the EU will require virtual asset service providers to cease offering trading services involving anonymous cryptocurrencies from July 2027, and will enforce strict identity verification for transactions exceeding €1,000 in private custodial wallets. This means that exchanges operating in the EU will be unable to list privacy coins such as Monero and ZEC. Furthermore, some EU member states have already implemented temporary measures: for example, the Belgian financial regulator required local traders to report any transactions involving anonymous cryptocurrencies in 2022 and recommended ceasing support. The French AMF has strict licensing regulations, and no privacy coins have yet been approved. The European law enforcement agency Europol has also frequently warned of the obstacles privacy coins pose to tracking criminal funds. This move by the EU has been criticized by some in the crypto industry, who argue that it infringes on individual privacy rights. However, from a regulatory perspective, the existence of anonymous cryptocurrencies does make the AML/CFT framework difficult to implement, and the FATF has also highlighted the risks of anonymity enhancement technologies in several reports. It can be expected that in the years leading up to the ban, the EU will gradually reduce the use of privacy coins through soft measures, such as classifying them as high-risk transactions and requiring increased due diligence, in preparation for 2027. This paves the way for a formal ban in 2022. Once the ban takes effect, the EU will become the next major jurisdiction after the US, Japan, and South Korea to exclude privacy coins. Currently, there are no laws in the US prohibiting the holding or trading of privacy coins, and most major compliant exchanges (such as Coinbase) do not support these coins based on their own risk control policies. However, US regulators are cracking down on privacy transactions through other means. For example, in 2022, the US Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) directly sanctioned the cryptocurrency mixing service Tornado Cash, a significant move targeting anonymization tools. Although Tornado Cash is not a token, this action sends a strong signal: the US does not tolerate completely anonymous crypto transaction channels. Many industry observers speculate that if privacy coins are widely used for illegal activities, the US may consider similar measures, such as adding specific privacy coin addresses to the sanctions list. In addition, the US Department of Justice and the IRS have invested in the development of on-chain analytics tools for privacy coins for many years, hoping to improve their investigative capabilities. Regarding exchanges, in 2019, US exchanges Kraken and ShapeShift... Some cryptocurrencies, such as Monero and Dash, have been delisted, either voluntarily or under duress, to avoid regulatory trouble. The US stance can be summarized as "technologically neutral but risk-oriented": not directly banning them through legislation, but rigorously investigating any abuse. Singapore also lacks explicit bans; the MAS primarily uses AML guidelines to require VASPs to conduct intensive reviews of anonymous transactions, prohibiting those that do not meet the requirements. This effectively makes it difficult for licensed institutions in Singapore to easily support privacy coins. In 2020, the major exchange Binance (which then operated in Singapore) stopped trading Monero and other cryptocurrencies. Others, such as Australia, are considering following the EU's lead, with reports suggesting the Australian government intends to ban the exchange of anonymous coins for fiat currency. In general, in jurisdictions without clear legislation, market forces have partially dictated their actions: mainstream compliant platforms generally avoid privacy coins to meet the compliance expectations of banks and regulators. The divergence in privacy coin regulation reflects the conflict between privacy rights and... The conflict of compliance. On the one hand, financial privacy is an individual right, and excessive financial surveillance may be considered an infringement; on the other hand, complete anonymity plunges law enforcement into darkness. A few countries (such as Switzerland) are discussing whether a compromise can be reached through methods such as mandating on-chain auditability, for example, requiring privacy coins to have "observation keys" for authorized users to view them, but there is no mature technical solution yet. Therefore, currently, countries tend to sacrifice the existence of privacy coins in exchange for the completeness of AML compliance. FATF Anonymity technology is also considered a high-risk factor in assessments of various countries, leading most compliant institutions to be reluctant to touch privacy coins. In this environment, the space for privacy coins to survive is shrinking, and the user base is moving underground or limited to high-tech geek circles. Cross-border impacts are also noteworthy: due to bans in Japan and the EU, privacy coin transactions are increasingly flowing to unregulated markets, creating a regulatory vacuum and the risk of transmission. International cooperation is expected to reduce this vulnerability, for example, countries jointly sharing intelligence on suspicious addresses and strengthening monitoring of fiat currency inflows and outflows. The overall prediction is that privacy coins will be restricted or even banned in more jurisdictions until a new technological solution that balances privacy and regulation emerges. Regulatory disagreements in this area are actually gradually narrowing, as previously hesitant Western countries are now following the stricter stances of East Asian countries. For investors and the industry, this means that privacy coins will find it difficult to integrate into mainstream finance, and related businesses must carefully consider regional compliance risks.

5.3 Regulatory Exploration of Real Asset Tokenization (RWA) and Decentralized Finance (DeFi)

Real Asset Tokenization (RWA)

Real Asset Tokenization (RWA) This refers to the process of representing and trading real-world assets (such as bonds, stocks, real estate, and commodities) in the form of blockchain tokens. Decentralized Finance (DeFi) is an ecosystem of applications that provide financial services (such as lending, trading, and derivatives) without intermediaries through smart contracts. RWA and DeFi are considered at the forefront of blockchain financial innovation, potentially bringing improved transaction efficiency and financial inclusion, but also posing significant challenges to traditional regulatory frameworks. How to incorporate these decentralized or cross-border new models into regulation is currently the most contentious issue among countries and is still under exploration. The United States adopts a strategy of primarily enforcing existing regulatory frameworks for RWA and DeFi. For RWA, as long as the token represents traditional financial assets (such as stocks and bonds), the SEC considers it a security, requiring it to comply with regulations for issuance and trading. For example, between 2018 and 2020, some platforms in the United States attempted to tokenize stocks (such as stock tokens on DX.Exchange and Uniswap), and the SEC... The SEC acted swiftly to halt these activities, deeming them violations of securities laws. Even if tokenized assets are physical assets (such as gold or real estate rights), if the public invests and expects returns, they may still be classified as securities by the SEC. Similarly, the CFTC focuses on tokenized commodity derivatives and has filed lawsuits against some DeFi protocols for commodity swap transactions. A typical example is the CFTC's 2022 lawsuit against Ooki DAO, the first enforcement action against a DeFi DAO, accusing it of operating an unlicensed leveraged trading platform, ultimately resulting in a default judgment that penalized the DAO's assets. The U.S. Treasury Department also released the "DeFi Illegal Financial Risk Assessment Report" in 2023, pointing out that many DeFi services did not comply with BSA obligations and that future regulation and legislation would be strengthened. On the other hand, there are also voices encouraging innovation in the U.S., such as Wyoming's legislation recognizing the legal status of DAOs and some members of Congress advocating for the establishment of a "sandbox" to provide space for compliance experimentation. However, at the federal level, there are no specific regulations for DeFi, and most activities fall into a gray area, with regulators "forcing" existing laws onto them. This has led to the situation where U.S. DeFi... Practitioners face significant compliance uncertainty, leading many projects to relocate overseas. Despite this, the US continues to use its long-arm jurisdiction to combat DeFi activities it deems illegal, such as sanctioning or prosecuting individuals involved in overseas coin mixing services and decentralized exchanges. It can be said that the US currently exhibits a strong enforcement-weak legislation stance regarding RWA/DeFi regulation. The EU's MiCA does not directly cover DeFi protocols and fully decentralized issuance activities (such as entityless DAOs), a deliberate legislative omission. EU officials have stated that they will assess how to regulate DeFi after the implementation of MiCA. In 2023, the European Commission published a research report on DeFi, favoring technology-driven regulation: monitoring DeFi activities through blockchain data (so-called "embedded regulation"), rather than entirely relying on traditional intermediary regulatory models. This approach stems from recommendations by experts such as the BIS and France, who suggest embedding regulatory nodes into DeFi networks to automatically collect necessary data. Legislatively, the EU may leverage the soon-to-be-established European Anti-Money Laundering Authority (AMLA) to implement certain AML measures on DeFi. Requirements, such as those for smart contracts with identifiable developers/controllers, stipulate that they must be registered as VASPs. This was already hinted at in the EU's draft Anti-Money Laundering Directive 6. Regarding RWA, the EU has launched a DLT pilot regulatory framework (Regulation (EU) 2022/858), allowing regulated institutions to issue and trade security tokens in a controlled sandbox environment. This pilot, implemented from March 2023 for six years, has already involved several exchanges and central banks, testing the on-chain issuance and trading of bonds and fund units.
